speaker: interviewer
text: You were walked into Krosno and the two rivers, and the Russian [INAUDIBLE], the German [INAUDIBLE], so go ahead. This is Tape number 2, and I'm interviewing Shlomo Berger, Los Angeles, California, USA. Go ahead, Shlomo.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, the-- the German, I don't know who it was, the commandant of town, whoever it was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let out a report, whatever. It was not actually an order. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how to explain that-- that all Jews, that they're willing to leave their homes and cross over to the Soviet side, the other side of the river, they'll be permitted to do that. They won't be stopped by the German army. The only thing is, no transportation will be provided, so you got to go by yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we made a decision. My younger brother and I, my younger brother Mike and I, that we're going back to the Soviet side. We decided whatever it is, it's always going to be better than-- seeing what we saw, it's going to be better than on the German side. My older brother Moses wouldn't go. My sister Berta with her husband and the two children wouldn't go.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents said, well, children, you go. You're young. We will stay here. There's no use to go and-- and uproot ourselves. Probably the-- the German will leave us alone, the old people. But you young people got to save yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we, the two of us, with a group of other young people and also a family [? Mintz ?], which were quite wealthy, and we were quite friendly with them. They hired a horse and buggy, and we loaded up about 15 people. And we went over to Sanok, to the river, crossed-- crossed the river. And the Germans let you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans did not say anything. We crossed the river not on a bridge. We had to cross it on-- in the water. We came in across the river to the Soviet side, to a little town, Lesko. Now we realized that the Soviets are completely different than the German. First, we didn't see any anti-Semitism. They treated us well. They greeted us fine. The only thing what they objected, that all refugees from the German side must leave the border sites. They have to get in, farther inside. So we went as far to a town, the first town that they permitted the refugees to stay was called Sambor. Now over there, everyone separated. My brother, the younger brother and I, Mike and I, we found a nice family. They took us into their house. They give us a place to sleep. But money we didn't have. We had to find some-- some place, something to do to-- to support ourselves. So we started to do a little handling. What they call it, black marketing, whatever it is. We used to go to Lwow and buy some stuff, bring it back to Sambor, and sell it to the Soviet soldiers. Anything they could get a hold of, they used to buy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now Lwow was under the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lwow was under the Russian side. Because the Soviets came in, they brought nothing into Poland. But anything that could get a hold of, they sent back to Russia. Rubles they had in tons. They paid any price they could get, materials, watches. Whatever they could get, they were buying, because the rubles weren't worth anything. One day my brother Mike and I were trying to go to the train in Sambor to go to Lwow to buy something. All of a sudden at the train station, somebody starts yelling. Shlomo! Mikhail! We turned around, and here is my brother Joshua, in a Polish uniform still. He was caught by the Russian-- by the Soviet Army, and they were taking him to the Soviet prison camps. Somehow he slipped away on the-- on the train station someplace east. Instead to go east, he went west. And he came to Sambor and we just met him. Now we are three of us already. And he-- as soon as he could, he threw away his uniform. We got some civilian clothes. And now the three of us are trying to do something to make a living. It came I think sometimes in December, 1939. It was very cold. The Soviets put in an announcement that they take in volunteers to go to work to the coal mines in Donbass, in-- I think in English it's called the Don-- at the Don locations, whatever it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my younger brother Mike and two of his friends went to Lwow. And they went to sign up to go to work to the coal mines. That was at the Eastern Ukraine, I think-- I think east of Kiev.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was short. Our family was very short, about 5'4'. The other two friends of his were tall guys, about six foot tall. They stayed all night in the frost and the cold, probably 15, 20 below zero, until in the morning, they came in to-- for registration to go to work in the coal mines. And the Soviet commissar happens to be a Jew. So the other two guys, they were tall, so he signed them up. When my brother came in, he says [NON-ENGLISH]. If you're going to be working two years underground in Donbass, you're going-- supposed to look like you look today. Go back home. It's not for you. So he picked himself up, came back to Sambor. But during the night that he was standing there, he got the pneumonia. He got pneumonia. And my brother Joshua and I didn't feel that we can handle a sick brother. The-- the borders were already closed between Soviet side and Germany. But smugglers were going back, back and forth. So we went, we hired a smuggler, and we smuggled my younger brother back home to Krosno, to recover. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All of a sudden a couple weeks later, or two, three weeks later, we found out that the Soviets don't want us there either, that everybody has to take an internal passport [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or you become a Soviet citizen or you go back to Germany or you become a refugee. Now we didn't want either. We didn't want to become Soviet citizen, because we didn't know how long it's going to last.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my older brother Joshua and I, with another group of young people from our town, we hired the same smuggler and we smuggled ourselves back to the German side. So in January 1940, we crossed the river San. It was all frozen, ice and snow. We crossed it. They loaded us out into a hiding house all ready on the German side. And the next day, we-- we-- they provided a horse and buggy, and we came back to Krosno.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came back to Krosno in January 1940, we saw the mistake that we did. It was the biggest mistake to go back to the German side. And we tried to make up our mind, we tried to convince our parents and the whole family to smuggle back to the Soviet side. No matter how bad it is, no use to stay in the German side, because it became terrible. We found out that all the Jews were ordered to wear white armbands with blue Stars of David, so they can be recognized, that any time a Jew will be caught without wearing an armband, they'll be shot. The German established Judenrats-- that means Jewish committees-- with presidents, with Jewish police. And all the orders that the German wanted to give, they gave it through the Judenrat. Jews had to go out every day to shovel the snow, to clean the streets, work on the airport, do all kind of work, without being paid. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Krosno, the president of the Judenrat was a man that used to live all his life in Germany. But in 1938, as a Polish citizen living in Germany, he got thrown out of Germany. His name was Engel. Now, he was actually Jewish, but in--in his behavior, in everything else, he was a Nazi German. Any time he used to talk to us, not like another Jew, but if he ordered something to do and you didn't want to do it, he used to call us the same way like the German used to called us, [NON-ENGLISH]. That means you dog pigs, or pig dogs. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I felt maybe I should make my life a little bit easier. My friends joined the police, Jewish police, so I also wanted to join the Jewish police. They didn't go out to work. They put somebody else to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I come home and I say, I'm going to join the Jewish police. And there became a revolution in my house. I can imagine.

speaker: interviewee
text: No way will you go and join the police. I says, why not? Because we don't want any collaborator, German collaborator in our house. If you want to go to join the-- the Jewish police, get out of the house. And I got convinced that they're right. And I accept that I'd rather go to work, being chased to work, then take somebody else to work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now the situation did not get any better. In 1940, something happened to me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that is worth mentioning in that story. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, since we had to work and we didn't get paid, we had to make some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1940, I was trying to do a little bit black marketing.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right to tell.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the easiest thing was to buy American dollars and sell American dollars for some profit, or keeping something for the future, because we knew the money isn't going to be worth anything. So I went to a town not far from Krakow, it's called Tarnow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had connections with a man over there which was an American dollar dealer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shlomo, may I ask you something? Wasn't it a risk to go from Krosno to another town and so on during the German--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was a risk. Any-- any step that you made was a risk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But since I was a risk-taker, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did it. While I was-- I made the connections. I arranged to-- to buy these American dollars. While I'm walking the street in Tarnow, I meet an acquaintance of mine that we trained together in 1937 for the Irgun. He was a boy from Tarnow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we greeted each other very friendly. We started to a conversation. And he says, Shlomo, what are you're doing here? I says, well listen, you know, I bought some American dollars, and I'm going to take them home, maybe make some money. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he asked me, what time are you going back home? I says, I'll be home on the afternoon train. I came in the afternoon to the train station, and here he was. He goes over to me, starts talking to me, here and there. We talk a little bit. And after he left, two civilian Gestapo men walk over to me and arrested me. Gee.

speaker: interviewee
text: That means I'm suspicious, that he was a collaborator and he pointed me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. They took me to the Gestapo, but I was-- while I was buying the dollars, I was smart enough and I didn't want to carry it with me. So I made arrangements with the dollar dealer. He had a 12-year-old girl, and she is going to go to the train together with me, and she will take that money all the way back to Krosno and go back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I was arrested by the Gestapo, I knew I had nothing. There was no proof that I have anything or something. It started to dawn to my mind that something is happening in here. So they take me to the Gestapo, and they started to-- to interview me. Where are the dollars that you bought? Now, there is no doubt already that he put-- that he-- that he pointed me out. I says, I don't have any dollars. I don't have the slightest dollars. I don't deal in any-- in any black market. I don't have anything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They beat me for two hours, until I got black and blue. And they left me lay on the floor, on the second floor over there. And they say, for today is enough. Tomorrow we'll start again. While they opened the door, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the door from a second office was open, and I see they got the girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they got the girl too. And I didn't know what is going to happen to her, or whatever it is. But I see they caught her, and I know-- I knew she has the money. But to leave this alone, after they left the office and they left me laying on the floor, I walked over to the window. The window was barred, but it had a circle in the middle. I was very skinny, weighed about 100 pounds, 105 pounds.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I tried through that circle in the middle of these bars whether my head and shoulders will go through. And I saw I can do it if I take off my-- my-- my coat. I took off my coat and I got over to the window, put my head in, and pulled my shoulders through, pulled myself out to the outside to the second floor, and jumped down from the second window and escaped. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know the town Tarnow. I didn't know where I'm going. I'm running, and I don't know where to go. And all of a sudden I see a girl there that's wearing an armband. There were no ghettos yet. So I stopped her, and I ask her whether she can take me home. And she take me-- she took me to her house. I told the parents that I escaped from the Gestapo. Everybody got scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put me on women clothes. They put me on a wig in my hat just in case I should look like a woman. And I told them to notify that man that his daughter was arrested, and if they have anything still in their house, they should clear it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the German, they never went to their house. They wanted the money. So they took the money away from the girl, and they let her go. They never came to search to their house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I stayed in that place over there for about four weeks, dressed in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tarnow, dressed in women clothes. And I notified through somebody-- I don't know how. I don't know. And I notified to my parents that where I-- that I am in Tarnow, not-- I did not give them no address where I am, but that I was arrested and escaped, and I'm hiding. My mother was very resourceful, so she found a guy over there that had connections with the Gestapo, paid off some money to him. And he went to the Gestapo, and he was supposed to arrange that they shouldn't be looking for me. And after she was told that everything is arranged, about four weeks later, I came back home to Krosno. When I came back to Krosno, the Gestapo was waiting for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They was-- they were waiting for me, and they re-arrested me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940. They re-arrested me. They didn't know what happened. All they knew that they had an order to-- that I was arrested and escaped. They didn't know why, where, and when. The Gestapo people knew me because their-- their office was two houses away from our shop. And they used to come into our shop all the time. My father used to do some-- some repairs for them and some-- some uniform helping for them. So they knew me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came back, there was a Gestapo man. His name was Becker. And he was waiting for me. He said, hey, Shlomo, get your coat. Let's go. And he took me in to the Gestapo office. They treated me quite well there, because they didn't know-- The Gestapo commandant asked me, what happened to you? Why did you run away? Why did you escape? I says-- what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, I don't know. I says, I was arrested and I don't know why. And-- and then after I got beaten up, I saw a possibility to run away, so I escaped. Anyway, he says, you're not going to escape anymore. Put on handcuffs, put me in a car, and he drove me to the next town, which was called Jaslo, about 20 kilometers away. They had over there a political prison, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for a Gestapo political prison. I was put in that prison in a cell together with approximately 10 or 12 Polish political prisoners. One of them was a Catholic priest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They did their prayers three days a-- therr three times a day, Catholic, Roman Catholic prayers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I learned during that time-- approximately six months I was there in prison, no interviews, no nothing. I learned everything what has to do with the Roman Catholic religion, what you do in the morning, how you cross yourself, what you-- what kind of prayer you say noontime, what kind of a prayer you say in the evening, what you do on Christmas, what do you do on-- what you do on Easter. So I was quite versed in the-- in the customs of the Catholic religion. And that was probably my salvation. I was in that prison approximately, I don't know, five, six months. I don't remember exactly. One day I'm being called to the office and here there is a German captain sitting there, not a Gestapo man. He was from-- from, I don't know, they called it a [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know what that means, probably the people that they are involved in-- in-- in foreign moneys. And he reads me a story. He reads me a story that I was buying American dollars in Tarnow. I was caught by the Gestapo. I was interviewed. I escaped, and I got re-arrested. And he gives me to sign the paper. I said, that's not true. I never did that. I didn't have any American dollars. I had nothing to do with it. He says, you're lying. For every lie you're going to say, you're going to get 25 over the back with rubber stick. He put me down, counted out 25 over my back.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, well. Are you going to sign that? I says, no, that's not true. He says you're going to get 25 for every lie. And he counted out four times 25, and I still would not admit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, he says-- he sees that he got a tough customer here, and he wanted to release me, because he was bought off. So he says to me, if you're gonna sing-- if you're not going to sign, he says, I'm going to right put you back to the cell. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I says what happens if I'm going to sign? He says, if I'm going to sign, he says, tomorrow I'm going to release you. Your mother is waiting for you outside the prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I believed him. I says, it's not true, but that's what you want me to sign, I will sign. I have no other choice.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I signed the confession. And he told the truth. The next day I was released. And my mother really was waiting outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me home. They found another [NON-ENGLISH], what they call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he got bought off. He-- he knew the story-- the story was actually true-- that I did not want to admit, but he released me. And that was the later part of 1940. Came back home. Things were much worse than they started with. Jews are being treated like dogs. They're being-- they're being taken to work. They're being killed without any reason. That Becker was actually a murderer. He-- according what we heard, he used to be a [NON-ENGLISH]. That means he was a man that-- that lived in Poland, but he was of German descent. And he was 1,000 times worse than an original German. Wearing a uniform, he was very proud of that Gestapo uniform. He used to come in to the Judenrat, and says, I need three Jews with a shovel. He used to take them down to the field. He told the guy with the shovel to dig a hole. He killed the other two, dumped them into the grave. Then he killed the third one. He took a fourth one to cover the grave. Now thinks to run away, there was no place to go. Jews could not take the trains anymore. Jews couldn't go any place. They-- they started to take away their businesses. They couldn't leave town. Any place they had they wanted to go, they had to register with the police wherever they have to go. Anyway, times were getting worse and worse and worse, until came 1941. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1941, I think it was June 1st, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or June 22nd, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany attacked the Soviet Union. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Germany attacked the Soviet Union. We were under the impression that the Soviet Union is so strong. And we were hopeful that finally going to come an end to the Germans. But Germany was so strong, and so well organized [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that it took no time and the German army was in front of Leningrad, in front of Moscow, within I think a month or two, in the same year. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Hitler established a fuhrerhauptquartiere. That means the-- the headquarters for the general command, approximately 15 kilometers from our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a kommandantur?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the headquarters for the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Headquarter. Ooh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because this was closed to--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russian border.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the former border. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And since we had an airport in our town, one day, 1941 sometimes-- July, August, I don't remember-- I see a lot of things going on in town. Small town, you can see so much police and Gestapo and soldiers, lining up the streets, on the roofs, all over to the main street.

speaker: interviewee
text: Me being the way I was, like they say in-- like they said in-- in the Polish or Yiddish, [NON-ENGLISH], a risk-taker, I wanted to see what is going to-- what's happening in here. And me with the armband on my-- on my-- on my coat, I get out to the marketplace to the main street among all these soldiers and police, and I'm standing and waiting, what's going to happen in here? What's going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden I hear from far away starting yelling, heil Hitler! Heil! Heil! [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Still don't know what's going on. Why, all of a sudden I see there's three Mercedes, steel Mercedes passing by, open Mercedes with bulletproofs. And in the middle one of the-- of the-- of the-- three cars, there's Hitler standing with his hand stretched out and greeting the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on right there.
